## 6. Data formats: what each file knows and forgets

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" collapse="true"}
A file format is a set of commitments about what information is preserved, what can be queried, and what has been discarded. No single file contains the complete history of a sequencing experiment or pangenome analysis.
:::

This section is not an exhaustive catalogue of genomics file formats. It focuses on formats that are directly relevant to this course, plus a small number of common sequencing formats needed to understand where the course files come from and what information they preserve or omit.

### General sequence, alignment, and variant formats

| Format | Contains | Role in this course | What to remember |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FASTA / FA** | DNA sequences without per-base quality | Input assemblies or haplotypes for WFMASH and PGGB; reference sequences for genotyping | Sequence names often become graph path identifiers. Preserve sample and haplotype naming conventions. |
| **FASTQ** | Read sequences and quality scores | Input reads for mapping and genotyping | The same syntax can represent different platforms, libraries, and assays. FASTQ does not retain the complete raw instrument signal. |
| **PAF** | Pairwise mappings between sequences | WFMASH output and all-versus-all alignments used during graph construction | PAF records mappings, not a graph. Optional tags determine whether detailed base-level alignment is available. |
| **SAM / BAM / CRAM** | Reads aligned to a linear reference or selected graph path; may also contain unmapped reads | Read mapping, QC, downstream analysis, and graph alignments projected onto a linear path | These formats do not preserve full graph topology. BAM/CRAM from a graph workflow may be a linear projection. |
| **VCF / BCF** | Variants, annotations, and sample genotypes relative to a reference | PanGenie input/output; POVU conversion from GFA to per-reference or combined VCF | VCF remains reference-relative even when variants originate from a pangenome graph. Reference path and normalization choices matter. |
| **gVCF** | Variant records plus confidence blocks for homozygous-reference or non-variant regions | Population and joint-genotyping exercises | gVCF is not simply another extension for an ordinary VCF. It also represents evidence that a region matches the reference or could not be confidently called. |
| **BED** | Genomic intervals and lightweight annotations | Regions, annotations, or path ranges used with ODGI and related tools | Coordinates require a named path or reference and a defined coordinate convention. A BED interval is not automatically graph-wide. |
| **GFF / GTF** | Structured genomic features and relationships | Gene, transcript, repeat, and regulatory annotation | Sequence names and coordinates must match the associated assembly or graph path. |

: {.format-table}

PacBio reads are also commonly distributed as unmapped or aligned BAM files with accompanying metadata. A `.bam` extension therefore does not by itself prove that reads have been aligned.

### Pangenome graph formats used in this course

GFA is the most common file format used to exchang pangenome graphs, much as FASTA is for exchanging linear sequences. Tools commonly convert GFA into optimized binary representations for computation.

| Format | Contains | Role in this course | What to remember |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GFA** | Graph segments, links, and embedded paths or walks | Main PGGB graph output and interchange with ODGI, POVU, and other graph tools | GFA is the principal text interchange format for pangenome graphs. Its version, path naming, and conventions still need to be documented. |
| **ODGI / OG** | ODGI's binary dynamic graph representation | Efficient graph manipulation, statistics, extraction, layout, and visualization | Built from GFA with `odgi build`. It is an optimized working format, not the main interchange format. |
| **rGFA** | GFA records with tags anchoring nodes to a selected linear reference | Reference-anchored graph exercises | Provides stable coordinates relative to one reference but gives that reference a privileged role. ODGI does not natively support rGFA. |
| **GBZ** | Compressed graph plus many embedded haplotype paths | Modern vg graph references and haplotype-aware mapping | Efficient for large path collections. It is not a replacement for GFA as a portable text interchange format. |

: {.format-table}


### Graph alignments and indexes

Graph alignments may be **surjected** onto a selected linear path and written as BAM or CRAM. That projection is convenient for conventional tools, but it no longer represents every possible route through the graph.

| Format | Contains | Associated use | What to remember |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GAF** | Text alignments of reads or sequences to graph paths | Portable graph-alignment interchange | Optional tags may carry base-level alignment details. |
| **GAM / GAMP** | Binary graph alignments or multipath alignments | Direct vg mapping and analysis | Rich but vg-specific formats. GAF is preferable when portable text interchange is required. |
| **XG** | Static indexed graph representation | vg graph queries and workflows that require an XG index | XG is a vg index, not an ODGI format. Some modern vg workflows instead use GBZ or other graph backends. |
| **GBWT** | Compressed haplotype-path index | vg haplotype-aware traversal and mapping | Stores paths and haplotypes; it is not a standalone general graph exchange format. |

: {.format-table}


### Reports, metadata, and companion files

| Format | Contains | Role in this course | What to remember |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TSV** | Command-specific tabular results | ODGI path tables, statistics, layouts, and annotations | Meaning depends on the column headers and command that generated the file. |
| **JSON** | Structured command-specific metadata or results | Script and visualization interchange where supported | Preserve the schema, software version, and command. JSON alone does not define biological meaning. |
| **Index files** | Lookup structures such as `.fai`, `.bai`, `.csi`, `.crai`, or `.tbi` | Rapid access to selected regions | An index is not a new biological data type. It must remain synchronized with its primary file. |

: {.format-table}

Raw instrument formats may appear earlier in a sequencing workflow: Illumina **BCL/intensity files** preserve cycle-level measurements, while nanopore **POD5/FAST5** files preserve electrical signal and metadata before basecalling. They remain important for reprocessing but are not central graph formats in the practical course.

### How the central course formats connect

```text
assemblies / haplotypes
        |
      FASTA
        |
        v
     WFMASH  ---------------------->  PAF alignments
        |                                  |
        +---------------+------------------+
                        v
                       PGGB
                        |
                        v
                 GFA pangenome graph
                        |
           +------------+----------------+
           |                             |
           v                             v
      odgi build                   graph/genotyping tools
           |                             |
           v                             +--> VCF (POVU, PanGenie)
      ODGI .og graph                     +--> GAF / GAM
           |                             +--> GBZ, XG, GBWT
           +--> TSV statistics/layout
           +--> PNG/SVG visualization

reads + reference confidence -----------------------> gVCF
```

::: {.callout-tip title="When looking at a genomic file, ask:"}

1. Which tool and command produced it?
2. Is it an interchange format, an index, or a tool-specific working file?
3. Which sample, haplotype, reference path, and coordinate system apply?
4. Which metadata, indexes, and companion files are required?
:::

---
